question: How do you estimate the minimum number of GPUs needed to serve a 70B parameter model in FP16?
choices: 1 GPU is always sufficient
Divide the model memory requirement (140 GB for FP16) by the GPU memory, accounting for KV cache and overhead. At minimum 2x 80 GB GPUs
The number of GPUs equals the number of model layers
The number of GPUs equals the number of attention heads
explanation: A 70B model in FP16 requires ~140 GB for weights alone. With 80 GB GPUs (like H100 or A100), you need at least 2 GPUs just for the weights. Adding KV cache and runtime overhead, you typically need 2-4 GPUs depending on batch size and sequence length. Using quantization (INT8 or INT4) can reduce the GPU requirement.

question: What is the advantage of the NVIDIA H100's Transformer Engine?
choices: It is a separate chip for running transformers
It automatically manages FP8 precision for transformer workloads, dynamically choosing between FP8 and FP16 per layer to maximize performance while maintaining accuracy
It replaces the need for CUDA cores
It only works with encoder models
explanation: The Transformer Engine on H100 GPUs intelligently manages precision on a per-layer, per-iteration basis. It automatically applies FP8 precision where it maintains accuracy and falls back to higher precision when needed, providing up to 4x speedup for transformer training compared to FP16 on the previous generation A100.

question: What is GPU memory fragmentation and how does it affect LLM serving?
choices: Physical damage to GPU memory chips
When available GPU memory is split into non-contiguous small blocks that cannot be used for large allocations like KV caches, reducing effective memory utilization
When GPU memory is completely full
An error in memory addressing
explanation: Memory fragmentation occurs when GPU memory becomes divided into small, non-contiguous free blocks. For LLM serving, KV caches traditionally require contiguous memory allocations. Fragmentation can prevent allocating new KV caches even when total free memory is sufficient, reducing the number of concurrent requests. PagedAttention addresses this problem.

question: What is the NVSwitch and what role does it play in multi-GPU systems?
choices: A software library for switching between GPU drivers
A high-bandwidth switch chip that enables all-to-all NVLink communication between all GPUs in a system, avoiding bottlenecks
A power management unit for GPUs
A display output connector
explanation: NVSwitch is a custom switch chip that connects all NVLink-enabled GPUs in a system with full bisection bandwidth, enabling any GPU to communicate with any other GPU at full NVLink speed simultaneously. In DGX systems, NVSwitch eliminates communication bottlenecks for tensor parallelism and other multi-GPU operations.

question: What is the difference between PCIe and NVLink bandwidth for GPU interconnects?
choices: PCIe is always faster than NVLink
NVLink provides significantly higher bandwidth (up to 900 GB/s on H100) compared to PCIe Gen5 (up to 128 GB/s bidirectional), making it critical for multi-GPU LLM workloads
They provide identical bandwidth
PCIe is only used for storage, not GPUs
explanation: NVLink provides dramatically higher GPU-to-GPU bandwidth than PCIe. On H100, NVLink provides up to 900 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth, roughly 7x faster than PCIe Gen5's ~128 GB/s. This bandwidth advantage is critical for tensor parallelism in LLM training and inference where GPUs must frequently exchange large amounts of data.

question: What is the NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD?
choices: A single GPU server
A large-scale AI infrastructure that connects multiple DGX systems with high-speed networking for enterprise-scale LLM training
A portable GPU device
A cloud-only service with no physical hardware
explanation: DGX SuperPOD is NVIDIA's scalable AI infrastructure solution that connects multiple DGX systems (e.g., 32 or more DGX H100 nodes) using InfiniBand networking. This creates a unified computing cluster with hundreds of GPUs capable of training the largest language models, with shared storage and cluster management capabilities.

question: When choosing between using 4 GPUs with tensor parallelism versus 4 GPUs with data parallelism for inference, what is the key consideration?
choices: They always produce the same results
Tensor parallelism reduces per-request latency by splitting computation across GPUs, while data parallelism increases throughput by processing separate requests on each GPU
Data parallelism always provides lower latency
Tensor parallelism requires no inter-GPU communication
explanation: Tensor parallelism splits a single model across GPUs, reducing per-request latency (each GPU handles part of each layer), but requires high-bandwidth communication between GPUs. Data parallelism runs complete model replicas on each GPU, serving separate requests simultaneously for higher throughput but with the same per-request latency.

question: What is the significance of memory bandwidth (measured in TB/s) for LLM inference performance?
choices: It only affects model loading time
It directly determines the token generation speed since the decode phase is memory-bandwidth-bound, requiring reading model weights for each token
It only matters during training
It has no impact on inference
explanation: During the decode phase of LLM inference, each token generation requires reading the model weights and KV cache from memory. With minimal computation per token, the speed of reading from memory (memory bandwidth) becomes the bottleneck. Higher bandwidth (like H100's 3.35 TB/s HBM3) directly translates to faster token generation.

question: What is the purpose of NVIDIA CUDA in the context of LLM deployment?
choices: It is a hardware component inside the GPU
A parallel computing platform and programming model that enables software to leverage NVIDIA GPUs for general-purpose computing including LLM workloads
A type of memory used in GPUs
A display technology
explanation: CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture) is NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and API that allows developers to use NVIDIA GPUs for general-purpose computing. All major deep learning frameworks use CUDA to accelerate training and inference operations, making it the foundation for running LLM workloads on NVIDIA GPUs.

question: How does GPU utilization differ between the prefill and decode phases of LLM inference?
choices: Both phases have identical GPU utilization
The prefill phase has high GPU compute utilization (processing many tokens in parallel), while the decode phase has low compute utilization (generating one token at a time)
The decode phase always has higher utilization
Neither phase uses the GPU significantly
explanation: During prefill, all input tokens are processed in parallel through matrix multiplications, achieving high compute utilization. During decode, only one new token is generated per step, resulting in small matrix-vector operations that underutilize the GPU's compute capacity. This is why batching multiple decode requests together is important for efficiency.

question: What role does the GPU-to-GPU bandwidth play in scaling LLM training across multiple nodes?
choices: It has no impact on multi-node training
Higher inter-node bandwidth (via InfiniBand) reduces communication overhead for gradient synchronization and parameter sharing, enabling efficient scaling
Only CPU-to-CPU bandwidth matters
Bandwidth only affects data loading
explanation: Multi-node LLM training requires frequent communication between GPUs across nodes for gradient synchronization (data parallelism) and parameter exchange (model parallelism). Higher inter-node bandwidth reduces the time spent on communication relative to computation, improving scaling efficiency. InfiniBand with RDMA provides the low-latency, high-bandwidth networking needed.

question: What is the concept of 'linear scaling' in distributed LLM training?
choices: Training only linear layers of the model
The ideal scenario where doubling the number of GPUs halves the training time, though real-world scaling is typically sub-linear due to communication overhead
Scaling the model linearly with data size
Using only linear algebra for training
explanation: Linear scaling means that adding more GPUs proportionally reduces training time (2x GPUs = 2x faster). In practice, communication overhead, synchronization barriers, and pipeline bubbles cause sub-linear scaling. Achieving near-linear scaling requires careful optimization of parallelism strategies, communication patterns, and computation-communication overlap.

question: What is the NVIDIA GH200 Grace Hopper Superchip?
choices: A standard desktop GPU
A combined CPU-GPU module connecting an ARM-based Grace CPU with an H100 GPU via a high-bandwidth coherent interface for AI workloads
A networking switch chip
A storage accelerator
explanation: The GH200 Grace Hopper Superchip combines NVIDIA's Grace ARM CPU and H100 GPU on a single module connected via NVLink-C2C with 900 GB/s coherent bandwidth. It provides up to 624 GB of unified memory (combining HBM3 and LPDDR5x) with a coherent memory model, ideal for large AI models that exceed single GPU memory.

question: What is the significance of FLOPS (Floating Point Operations Per Second) when evaluating GPUs for LLM training?
choices: FLOPS measure memory capacity
FLOPS measure the GPU's raw computational throughput, indicating how quickly it can perform the matrix operations that dominate LLM training
FLOPS measure network bandwidth
FLOPS measure power consumption
explanation: FLOPS indicate a GPU's computational power for performing floating-point operations. LLM training is dominated by matrix multiplications, so higher FLOPS translates to faster processing of these operations. For example, the H100 provides ~989 TFLOPS for FP16 Tensor Core operations, roughly 3x the A100's ~312 TFLOPS.

question: Why is ECC (Error Correcting Code) memory important for LLM training on GPUs?
choices: It increases memory bandwidth
It detects and corrects memory bit errors that could corrupt model weights or gradients during long training runs spanning days or weeks
It reduces memory capacity
It is only needed for gaming GPUs
explanation: LLM training runs last days to weeks, during which random memory bit flips (caused by cosmic rays or electrical noise) could silently corrupt training data, weights, or gradients. ECC memory detects and corrects these errors, ensuring training reliability. Professional GPUs (A100, H100) include ECC memory, while consumer GPUs typically do not.

question: What is the approximate total GPU memory of an 8-GPU DGX H100 system?
choices: 80 GB
320 GB
640 GB
explanation: A DGX H100 system contains 8 H100 SXM GPUs, each with 80 GB of HBM3 memory. The total GPU memory is 8 x 80 GB = 640 GB. This combined memory is critical for training and serving large language models, with NVLink Switch enabling unified access patterns across all GPUs.

question: What is the impact of GPU thermal throttling on LLM training?
choices: It improves training speed
The GPU reduces clock speeds when overheating, slowing down training and potentially causing inconsistent training times across GPUs in a cluster
It has no effect on performance
It only affects inference, not training
explanation: When GPUs overheat beyond their thermal limits, they automatically reduce clock speeds (throttle) to prevent hardware damage. This reduces computational throughput, slowing training. In distributed training, one throttling GPU can slow down the entire cluster if synchronization is required, making proper cooling and airflow critical in GPU data centers.

question: What is the advantage of using InfiniBand with GPUDirect RDMA for multi-node LLM training?
choices: It reduces GPU memory requirements
It enables direct data transfer between GPUs on different nodes without involving the CPU, reducing latency and freeing CPU resources
It increases model accuracy
It eliminates the need for NVLink within a node
explanation: GPUDirect RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) allows direct data transfer between GPU memory on one node and GPU memory on another node via InfiniBand, bypassing the CPU and system memory entirely. This reduces latency, increases bandwidth utilization, and frees the CPU for other tasks, which is critical for efficient gradient synchronization in distributed training.

question: What is the relationship between model size and the number of GPUs required for training, given the Adam optimizer?
choices: The number of GPUs only depends on the dataset size
Each parameter requires approximately 18-20 bytes during training (FP16 weights + FP32 master weights + FP32 optimizer states + FP16 gradients), so memory requirements scale linearly with model size
Larger models always need fewer GPUs
The number of GPUs is fixed regardless of model size
explanation: When training with mixed precision and the Adam optimizer, each parameter requires: 2 bytes (FP16 weights) + 4 bytes (FP32 master copy) + 8 bytes (FP32 Adam momentum and variance) + 2 bytes (FP16 gradients) = ~16-20 bytes. A 70B model thus needs ~1.1-1.4 TB just for these, requiring distributed training across many GPUs.

question: What is the NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM's in-flight batching feature and why is it important for GPU utilization?
choices: It batches model weights for compression
It allows new requests to be added to a running inference batch and completed requests to exit, maximizing GPU compute utilization across requests with varying lengths
It batches GPU driver updates
It groups similar prompts together
explanation: TensorRT-LLM's in-flight batching (continuous batching) inserts new requests into a running batch as others complete. Without this, the GPU idles while waiting for the longest sequence in a static batch to finish. In-flight batching keeps the GPU busy with a dynamic set of requests, dramatically improving throughput and GPU utilization.

question: What is the primary purpose of NVLink Switch in the DGX H100 architecture?
choices: To connect the DGX system to the internet
To provide all-to-all full-bandwidth NVLink connectivity between all 8 GPUs in the system, enabling efficient multi-GPU communication
To switch between different GPU driver versions
To manage power distribution to GPUs
explanation: NVLink Switch in DGX H100 connects all 8 GPUs via fourth-generation NVLink, providing 900 GB/s bidirectional bandwidth per GPU. It enables any GPU to communicate with any other GPU at full bandwidth simultaneously, which is essential for tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism, and collective operations used in LLM training and inference.

question: What are the key factors to consider when sizing a GPU cluster for LLM pre-training?
choices: Only the number of CPU cores matters
Model size (parameter count), desired training time, training data size, inter-GPU/inter-node bandwidth, and memory capacity per GPU
Only the total disk storage capacity
Only the number of GPUs without regard to their specifications
explanation: Sizing a GPU cluster requires considering: total GPU memory needed for model parameters, optimizer states, and activations; compute throughput (FLOPS) to achieve the target training time; inter-GPU bandwidth (NVLink) and inter-node bandwidth (InfiniBand) for parallelism efficiency; and data throughput for feeding the training pipeline. All these factors interact and must be balanced.

question: Which TWO components are essential parts of the transformer architecture used in modern LLMs? (Select TWO)
choices: Self-attention mechanism
Recurrent hidden state loops
Positional encoding
Convolutional pooling layers
Decision tree ensembles
explanation: The transformer architecture relies on self-attention mechanisms to capture relationships between all tokens in a sequence, and positional encoding to inject information about token order since transformers process all positions in parallel rather than sequentially.

question: Which TWO are parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques that avoid updating all model weights? (Select TWO)
choices: Full fine-tuning of all layers
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
Training from random initialization
Prompt tuning / prefix tuning
Increasing the model's hidden dimension
explanation: LoRA injects small trainable low-rank matrices into existing layers, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters. Prompt tuning and prefix tuning prepend learnable soft tokens to the input, keeping the base model frozen. Both are PEFT methods.

question: Which TWO techniques are commonly used to reduce LLM inference latency and memory footprint? (Select TWO)
choices: Increasing model parameter count
Quantization (e.g., INT8 or INT4)
Disabling GPU acceleration
KV-cache optimization
Training for more epochs
explanation: Quantization reduces precision of model weights (e.g., FP16 to INT8) to lower memory usage and speed up computation. KV-cache optimization avoids recomputing key-value pairs for previously generated tokens during autoregressive generation, significantly reducing latency.

question: Which TWO components are required to implement a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline? (Select TWO)
choices: A vector database or embedding store for document retrieval
A reinforcement learning reward model
An LLM that generates responses using retrieved context
A GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) for data augmentation
A separate model for image classification
explanation: RAG combines retrieval and generation: a vector store holds document embeddings for semantic search, and an LLM generates answers grounded in the retrieved context. This reduces hallucination and allows the model to access up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge.

question: Which TWO parallelism strategies are used to train LLMs that exceed single-GPU memory capacity? (Select TWO)
choices: Tensor parallelism (splitting layers across GPUs)
Running the model on CPU only
Pipeline parallelism (splitting model stages across GPUs)
Reducing the dataset to fit in one GPU
Using smaller batch sizes without any parallelism
explanation: Tensor parallelism splits individual layers (e.g., attention heads or MLP columns) across GPUs for intra-layer parallelism. Pipeline parallelism splits the model into sequential stages across GPUs, with micro-batches flowing through the pipeline. Both are essential for training models that exceed single-GPU memory.

question: Which TWO techniques are used during the pre-training phase of large language models? (Select TWO)
choices: Next-token prediction (causal language modeling)
Supervised classification on labeled datasets
Masked language modeling (predicting masked tokens)
Reinforcement learning from human feedback
K-nearest neighbor retrieval
explanation: Causal language modeling (next-token prediction) is used by decoder-only models like GPT. Masked language modeling (predicting randomly masked tokens) is used by encoder models like BERT. Both are self-supervised pre-training objectives that learn language representations from unlabeled text.

question: Which TWO factors most directly determine the computational cost of training an LLM? (Select TWO)
choices: The number of model parameters
The programming language used to write the training script
The size of the training dataset (number of tokens)
The color depth of any images in the dataset
The geographic location of the data center
explanation: Training compute (measured in FLOPs) scales roughly as 6 × N × D, where N is the number of parameters and D is the number of training tokens. These two factors are the primary drivers of training cost according to scaling laws research.

question: Which TWO are common evaluation benchmarks used to assess LLM capabilities? (Select TWO)
choices: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)
ImageNet (image classification accuracy)
HumanEval (code generation)
BLEU score for image captioning only
PageRank for web search ranking
explanation: MMLU tests knowledge across 57 academic subjects and is a standard benchmark for general LLM reasoning. HumanEval tests code generation by evaluating whether generated functions pass unit tests. Both are widely used to compare LLM capabilities.

question: Which TWO are key steps in the RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) alignment process? (Select TWO)
choices: Training a reward model from human preference comparisons
Removing all pre-training weights before alignment
Using PPO or similar RL algorithms to optimize the policy against the reward model
Converting the model from transformer to RNN architecture
Reducing the vocabulary size to 1000 tokens
explanation: RLHF involves: (1) collecting human preference data and training a reward model that scores responses, then (2) using a reinforcement learning algorithm like PPO to fine-tune the LLM to maximize the reward model's score while staying close to the supervised fine-tuned policy.

question: Which TWO strategies help prevent catastrophic forgetting when fine-tuning a pre-trained LLM? (Select TWO)
choices: Using a very high learning rate to overwrite old knowledge quickly
Using a low learning rate relative to pre-training
Training on the fine-tuning dataset for hundreds of epochs
Mixing a portion of the original pre-training data with fine-tuning data
Removing the attention layers before fine-tuning
explanation: A low learning rate makes small updates that adapt the model without drastically overwriting pre-trained knowledge. Mixing pre-training data into the fine-tuning dataset (replay) helps the model retain general capabilities while learning the new task.

question: Which TWO data quality practices are critical when preparing a fine-tuning dataset for an LLM? (Select TWO)
choices: Deduplicating training examples to avoid memorization bias
Including as much noisy data as possible for robustness
Ensuring diverse and representative examples across the target distribution
Using only examples shorter than 10 tokens
Encrypting all training data so the model cannot read it
explanation: Deduplication prevents the model from memorizing repeated examples and improves generalization. Diversity and representativeness ensure the model learns the full range of desired behaviors rather than overfitting to a narrow subset of patterns.

question: Which TWO serving strategies help handle high-throughput LLM inference in production? (Select TWO)
choices: Continuous batching (inflight batching) of requests
Processing one request at a time sequentially
Speculative decoding with a smaller draft model
Disabling the GPU and using CPU-only inference
Limiting the model to a single output token per request
explanation: Continuous batching dynamically adds new requests to an in-progress batch as earlier requests finish, maximizing GPU utilization. Speculative decoding uses a faster draft model to propose multiple tokens that the larger model verifies in parallel, increasing tokens per second.

question: Which TWO metrics are most important when evaluating LLM inference performance in production? (Select TWO)
choices: Time to first token (TTFT) latency
Number of lines of code in the serving framework
Throughput measured in tokens per second
The file size of the server's configuration file
The number of Python packages installed
explanation: TTFT measures how quickly a user sees the first generated token, critical for interactive applications. Throughput (tokens/second) determines how many concurrent users the system can serve. Together they define the user experience and cost efficiency of an LLM deployment.

question: Which TWO are benefits of serving LLMs with NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM? (Select TWO)
choices: Optimized CUDA kernels for transformer operations like attention
Automatic conversion of LLMs to image classification models
Support for in-flight batching and paged KV-cache
Elimination of the need for GPUs entirely
Built-in web browser for model testing
explanation: TensorRT-LLM provides highly optimized CUDA kernels for transformer operations including flash attention and fused MLP layers. It also supports in-flight batching and paged KV-cache for efficient memory management, significantly improving inference throughput and latency.

question: Which TWO techniques improve the quality of retrieval in a RAG system? (Select TWO)
choices: Using hybrid search combining dense embeddings and sparse keyword matching
Retrieving documents randomly without any relevance scoring
Chunking documents into semantically meaningful segments with overlap
Using a fixed chunk size of exactly one character
Ignoring metadata and relying only on document titles
explanation: Hybrid search combines semantic (dense embedding) similarity with keyword (sparse BM25) matching for better recall across different query types. Intelligent chunking with overlap ensures that relevant context isn't split across chunk boundaries, improving retrieval precision.

question: Which TWO are common failure modes that RAG is specifically designed to mitigate in LLM applications? (Select TWO)
choices: Hallucination of facts not present in training data
Slow GPU memory bandwidth
Inability to access up-to-date or domain-specific knowledge
High electricity costs for inference
Difficulty in tokenizing non-English text
explanation: RAG mitigates hallucination by grounding responses in retrieved documents rather than relying solely on parametric memory. It also addresses the knowledge cutoff problem by allowing the model to access current, domain-specific, or proprietary information at query time.

question: Which TWO interconnect technologies are critical for multi-GPU and multi-node LLM training? (Select TWO)
choices: NVLink for high-bandwidth intra-node GPU-to-GPU communication
USB 2.0 for connecting GPUs to the motherboard
InfiniBand for high-bandwidth inter-node communication
Bluetooth for wireless GPU synchronization
SATA for GPU memory expansion
explanation: NVLink provides high-bandwidth, low-latency communication between GPUs within a single node (up to 900 GB/s on H100). InfiniBand (e.g., NDR 400 Gb/s) provides the high-bandwidth, low-latency fabric needed for efficient gradient synchronization across nodes in large training clusters.

question: Which TWO memory optimization techniques allow training larger models on limited GPU memory? (Select TWO)
choices: Gradient checkpointing (activation recomputation)
Increasing batch size without any memory management
Mixed-precision training (FP16/BF16 with FP32 master weights)
Using only CPU RAM and disabling GPU memory entirely
Storing all optimizer states in full FP64 precision
explanation: Gradient checkpointing trades compute for memory by recomputing activations during the backward pass instead of storing them all. Mixed-precision training uses FP16/BF16 for forward/backward passes while maintaining FP32 master weights, nearly halving activation and gradient memory.

question: Which TWO features of the NVIDIA H100 GPU make it particularly suited for LLM workloads? (Select TWO)
choices: Transformer Engine with FP8 support for faster attention computation
Built-in web server for hosting models
80 GB of HBM3 memory with high bandwidth
Integrated display output for visualization
Automatic code generation without any software
explanation: The H100's Transformer Engine automatically manages mixed FP8/FP16 precision for transformer layers, dramatically accelerating attention and MLP operations. Its 80 GB of HBM3 memory with 3.35 TB/s bandwidth allows fitting larger model shards and batch sizes per GPU.

question: Which TWO guardrail techniques help ensure safe and reliable outputs from LLM applications in production? (Select TWO)
choices: Input/output content filtering to block harmful or off-topic responses
Removing all system prompts to give the model full freedom
Structured output validation (e.g., JSON schema enforcement)
Disabling all logging to improve latency
Allowing the model to execute arbitrary system commands
explanation: Content filtering scans inputs and outputs for harmful, toxic, or off-topic content to prevent misuse. Structured output validation ensures the model returns data in the expected format (e.g., valid JSON matching a schema), preventing downstream errors in application pipelines.

question: What is the computational complexity of the standard self-attention mechanism with respect to sequence length n?
choices: O(n^2) in both time and memory
O(n) in time, O(n^2) in memory
O(n log n) in both time and memory
O(n) in both time and memory
explanation: Standard self-attention computes pairwise attention scores between all tokens, resulting in O(n^2) complexity for both time and memory. This quadratic scaling is the primary bottleneck for processing long sequences in transformer models.

question: Which activation function is most commonly used in the feed-forward layers of modern LLMs like LLaMA and PaLM?
choices: ReLU
Sigmoid
Tanh
SwiGLU (or variants of GLU)
explanation: Modern LLMs such as LLaMA and PaLM use SwiGLU or similar Gated Linear Unit variants in their FFN layers. SwiGLU combines the Swish activation with a gating mechanism, providing better training dynamics and performance compared to standard ReLU.

question: What does 'perplexity' measure when evaluating a language model?
choices: The number of parameters in the model
The speed of inference in tokens per second
How well the model predicts a held-out test set, with lower values indicating better performance
The amount of GPU memory required for training
explanation: Perplexity is the exponentiation of the average cross-entropy loss on a test set. It measures how surprised the model is by the test data, with lower perplexity indicating the model assigns higher probabilities to the actual next tokens.

question: In Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), how is positional information encoded?
choices: By adding sinusoidal vectors to input embeddings
By learning absolute position embeddings during training
By applying rotation matrices to query and key vectors based on their position
By concatenating position IDs to token embeddings
explanation: RoPE encodes position by applying rotation matrices to the query and key vectors in attention. This allows the dot product between queries and keys to naturally depend on their relative positions, enabling better length generalization than absolute positional encodings.

question: What is Grouped Query Attention (GQA) designed to improve compared to standard Multi-Head Attention?
choices: It increases the model's parameter count for better accuracy
It eliminates the need for positional encodings
It replaces attention with convolutional operations
It reduces KV-cache memory usage by sharing key-value heads across query heads
explanation: Grouped Query Attention groups multiple query heads to share a single set of key-value heads, reducing the KV-cache size during inference. This provides a middle ground between Multi-Head Attention and Multi-Query Attention.

question: What distinguishes a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformer from a standard dense transformer?
choices: MoE activates only a subset of expert FFN layers per token via a gating mechanism
MoE models use convolutional layers instead of attention
MoE models have fewer total parameters than dense models
MoE models do not require GPU acceleration
explanation: In a Mixture-of-Experts transformer, each token is routed to only a subset of available expert FFN layers by a learned gating network. This allows the model to have many more total parameters while keeping the per-token computation cost comparable to a smaller dense model.

question: What is the purpose of the embedding layer in a transformer-based language model?
choices: To convert discrete token IDs into continuous dense vector representations
To perform the final classification of output tokens
To normalize the attention scores
To apply dropout regularization
explanation: The embedding layer maps each discrete token ID from the vocabulary to a dense, continuous vector in a high-dimensional space. These embeddings serve as the input representations that the transformer layers then process through attention and feed-forward operations.

question: What is the key innovation of FlashAttention compared to standard attention implementations?
choices: It uses approximate attention with random projections
It replaces attention with linear recurrence
It prunes attention heads during inference
It tiles the computation to minimize HBM reads/writes, achieving IO-aware exact attention
explanation: FlashAttention is an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that tiles the attention computation into blocks that fit in GPU SRAM, dramatically reducing the number of reads and writes to high-bandwidth memory (HBM). This yields significant speedups without any approximation.

question: What problem does the residual connection (skip connection) solve in deep transformer models?
choices: It reduces the vocabulary size
It prevents the vanishing gradient problem by allowing gradients to flow directly through the network
It eliminates the need for layer normalization
It compresses the model for deployment
explanation: Residual connections add the input of a sub-layer directly to its output, creating a shortcut path for gradient flow during backpropagation. This mitigates the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks and makes it easier to train transformers with many layers.

question: What is 'beam search' in LLM text generation?
choices: A training algorithm for optimizing model weights
A hardware optimization for parallel GPU execution
A decoding strategy that maintains multiple candidate sequences and selects the highest-scoring one
A method for compressing model parameters
explanation: Beam search explores multiple candidate output sequences simultaneously by keeping the top-k most probable partial sequences at each step. It tends to produce more coherent and higher-likelihood text than greedy decoding, though at increased computational cost.

question: Which scaling law relationship, as described by Kaplan et al. and Chinchilla, governs LLM performance?
choices: Loss decreases as a power law with increases in model parameters, dataset size, and compute
Performance scales logarithmically with GPU count only
Performance depends only on model architecture, not scale
Doubling parameters always halves the loss
explanation: Neural scaling laws show that LLM loss follows a power-law relationship with model size, dataset size, and compute budget. The Chinchilla study further showed that optimal performance requires scaling data and parameters together, not just increasing model size.

question: What is the KV-cache in autoregressive LLM inference?
choices: A cache that stores compiled CUDA kernels for reuse
A file system cache for storing model checkpoints
A stored copy of previously computed key and value tensors to avoid redundant computation during token generation
A cache for frequently accessed vocabulary embeddings
explanation: During autoregressive generation, each new token needs to attend to all previous tokens. The KV-cache stores the key and value projections from prior steps so they do not need to be recomputed, significantly speeding up sequential token generation.

question: What does RMSNorm improve upon compared to standard LayerNorm?
choices: It adds more learnable parameters for better expressiveness
It processes tokens in parallel across multiple GPUs
It removes the mean-centering step, reducing computation while maintaining training stability
It replaces normalization with dropout
explanation: RMSNorm simplifies LayerNorm by removing the mean-centering operation and only normalizing by the root mean square of activations. This reduces computational overhead while empirically maintaining the same training stability benefits as full LayerNorm.

question: What is the difference between greedy decoding and sampling in LLM text generation?
choices: Greedy decoding randomly selects tokens while sampling picks the most likely token
There is no difference between the two methods
Greedy decoding is used only during training, sampling only during inference
Greedy decoding always selects the most probable token at each step, while sampling draws from the probability distribution
explanation: Greedy decoding deterministically picks the highest-probability token at each step, which can lead to repetitive text. Sampling draws tokens according to their probability distribution (often modified by temperature), producing more diverse and natural-sounding text.

question: What is a 'hallucination' in the context of LLM outputs?
choices: A visual artifact in image generation models
A GPU memory error that corrupts output
When the model generates factually incorrect or fabricated information that sounds plausible
A network latency issue causing garbled text
explanation: Hallucination refers to LLM outputs that contain confidently stated but factually wrong or entirely fabricated information. This occurs because LLMs generate text based on learned patterns rather than verified knowledge, making them prone to producing plausible-sounding but incorrect claims.

question: What is the purpose of the unembedding (language model head) layer at the output of a decoder-only LLM?
choices: To convert the final hidden state back into a probability distribution over the vocabulary for predicting the next token
To compress the model for faster inference
To apply the attention mechanism one final time
To encode positional information
explanation: The unembedding layer projects the final hidden state vector to a vector of vocabulary size, which is then passed through softmax to produce a probability distribution over all possible next tokens. It often shares weights with the input embedding layer.

question: What is 'multi-query attention' (MQA)?
choices: An attention mechanism that processes multiple user queries simultaneously
An attention variant where all query heads share a single key-value head, greatly reducing KV-cache memory during inference
An attention mechanism that queries an external database
A method for parallelizing attention across multiple GPUs
explanation: Multi-query attention uses multiple query heads but only a single shared key-value head, dramatically reducing the KV-cache size during autoregressive inference. While this can slightly impact model quality compared to full multi-head attention, the memory savings are substantial.

question: What is the cross-entropy loss function used in LLM pre-training?
choices: The difference between predicted and actual token counts
The squared error between predicted and target embeddings
The negative log probability of the correct next token under the model's predicted distribution
The cosine distance between input and output vectors
explanation: Cross-entropy loss measures how well the model's predicted probability distribution matches the true distribution. It computes the negative log probability assigned to the correct token, penalizing the model more when it assigns low probability to the right answer.

question: What does 'autoregressive generation' mean in the context of LLMs?
choices: The model generates all output tokens simultaneously in one step
The model generates tokens one at a time, with each new token conditioned on all previously generated tokens
The model generates text by looking up entries in a database
The model generates text backwards from the last token to the first
explanation: Autoregressive generation produces output tokens sequentially, one at a time. Each new token is generated by conditioning on the full sequence of previously generated tokens plus the input prompt. This sequential dependency is why LLM inference has a memory-bandwidth-bound decode phase.

question: What is the feed-forward network (FFN) component in a transformer block responsible for?
choices: Computing attention scores between tokens
Encoding positional information
Generating the final output probabilities
Applying a non-linear transformation to each token independently, expanding and then projecting back the representation
explanation: The FFN in each transformer block consists of two linear transformations with a non-linear activation in between. It processes each token position independently, typically expanding the representation to 4x the hidden dimension before projecting back.

question: What is the 'scaling factor' in scaled dot-product attention and why is it used?
choices: A factor of 1/n to normalize by sequence length
A learned parameter that adapts during training
A factor of 1/sqrt(d_k) to prevent dot products from growing too large with high dimensions
A factor of 2 to double the attention resolution
explanation: The dot products of queries and keys grow in magnitude with the dimension d_k, potentially pushing softmax into regions of extremely small gradients. Dividing by sqrt(d_k) counteracts this effect, keeping the softmax function in a more numerically stable range.

question: What is the 'attention mask' used for in decoder-only transformer models during training?
choices: To speed up matrix multiplication on GPUs
To prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions, enforcing causal left-to-right generation
To mask out padding tokens in variable-length batches only
To randomly mask tokens for data augmentation
explanation: The causal attention mask ensures that each position can only attend to itself and earlier positions. This prevents information leakage from future tokens during training, maintaining the autoregressive property needed for left-to-right text generation.

question: What is the primary advantage of LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) over full fine-tuning?
choices: LoRA freezes the pre-trained weights and injects small trainable low-rank matrices, drastically reducing trainable parameters and memory
LoRA trains all parameters for maximum accuracy
LoRA eliminates the need for any training data
LoRA only works with encoder-only models
explanation: LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices that are much smaller than the original weight matrices. By keeping the pre-trained weights frozen and only training these small matrices, LoRA reduces trainable parameters by orders of magnitude while achieving comparable performance.

question: In RLHF, what is the role of the reward model?
choices: To generate training data for pre-training
To directly modify the LLM's weights through backpropagation
To tokenize input text before it reaches the LLM
To score model outputs based on learned human preferences, guiding the policy optimization
explanation: The reward model in RLHF is trained on human preference data to predict which outputs humans prefer. It then provides scalar reward signals used by algorithms like PPO to fine-tune the LLM's policy toward generating preferred responses.

question: What is Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and how does it differ from RLHF?
choices: DPO is a pre-training technique that replaces next-token prediction
DPO eliminates the need for a separate reward model by directly optimizing the policy using preference pairs
DPO uses only supervised learning with labeled classification data
DPO requires more compute than RLHF because it trains two models simultaneously
explanation: DPO reformulates the RLHF objective to directly optimize the language model using preference pairs without training a separate reward model. This simplifies the alignment pipeline, reduces computational cost, and avoids the instability often associated with reinforcement learning.

question: What is the purpose of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) in the LLM alignment pipeline?
choices: To teach the model new languages it was not pre-trained on
To reduce the model's parameter count
To train the model on high-quality instruction-response pairs so it learns to follow instructions
To convert the model from dense to sparse architecture
explanation: Supervised fine-tuning trains the model on curated datasets of instruction-response pairs, teaching it to follow user instructions and generate helpful responses. SFT typically occurs after pre-training and before RLHF in the standard alignment pipeline.

question: What is mixed-precision training and why is it widely used for LLMs?
choices: Training with a mix of different model architectures
Alternating between CPU and GPU during training
Using FP16/BF16 for forward/backward passes while keeping FP32 master weights, reducing memory and increasing throughput
Using different learning rates for different layers
explanation: Mixed-precision training performs most computations in half-precision (FP16 or BF16) while maintaining a copy of the weights in FP32 for gradient accumulation. This nearly halves memory usage for activations and gradients and leverages Tensor Cores for faster computation.

question: What does the AdamW optimizer improve upon compared to standard Adam?
choices: It uses a constant learning rate instead of adaptive rates
It eliminates the need for momentum terms
It only updates weights when the gradient exceeds a threshold
It decouples weight decay from the gradient update, providing proper L2 regularization
explanation: AdamW decouples weight decay from the adaptive gradient computation, applying it directly to the weights rather than through the gradient. This provides more effective regularization and has become the standard optimizer for training large language models.

question: What is data parallelism in the context of distributed LLM training?
choices: Splitting the model across multiple GPUs, with each GPU holding a portion
Using multiple data formats (FP16, FP32) simultaneously
Preprocessing data on CPUs while training on GPUs
Replicating the entire model on each GPU and splitting the training data across GPUs
explanation: In data parallelism, each GPU holds a complete copy of the model and processes different mini-batches of data. Gradients are synchronized across GPUs (typically via all-reduce) before each weight update, enabling faster training by processing more data per step.

question: What is NVIDIA NeMo primarily used for?
choices: A GPU monitoring and thermal management tool
A framework for building, training, and deploying large language models and other generative AI models
A graphics rendering engine for real-time visualization
A network protocol for GPU-to-GPU communication
explanation: NVIDIA NeMo is an end-to-end framework for developing generative AI models, including LLMs, speech, and multimodal models. It provides tools for data curation, distributed training, model customization, and deployment optimization within the NVIDIA ecosystem.

question: What is the catastrophic forgetting problem in LLM fine-tuning?
choices: When training data is lost due to storage failures
When the learning rate is set too high and training diverges
When fine-tuning on a new task causes the model to lose previously learned knowledge and capabilities
When the model runs out of GPU memory during training
explanation: Catastrophic forgetting occurs when fine-tuning a model on new data or tasks overwrites the knowledge learned during pre-training or prior fine-tuning stages. PEFT methods like LoRA help mitigate this by keeping the original weights frozen.

question: What distinguishes QLoRA from standard LoRA?
choices: QLoRA uses larger rank matrices for better performance
QLoRA is designed only for encoder models
QLoRA eliminates the need for any adapter matrices
QLoRA quantizes the base model to 4-bit while training LoRA adapters in higher precision, reducing memory further
explanation: QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization of the frozen base model with standard LoRA adapter training in BF16. This allows fine-tuning a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU while maintaining comparable performance to full 16-bit fine-tuning.

question: What is the purpose of a learning rate warmup schedule in LLM training?
choices: To gradually increase the learning rate from a small value at the start of training to the target rate
To cool down the GPU temperature during training
To increase the batch size over time
To reduce the number of training epochs needed
explanation: Learning rate warmup starts training with a very small learning rate and gradually increases it to the peak value over a specified number of steps. This prevents large, destabilizing gradient updates early in training when the model weights are still randomly initialized.

question: What does the 'gradient accumulation' technique achieve in LLM training?
choices: It stores gradients on CPU to save GPU memory
It accumulates gradients over multiple mini-batches before performing a weight update, simulating a larger effective batch size
It removes gradients below a certain threshold
It converts gradients from FP32 to INT8
explanation: Gradient accumulation sums gradients over multiple forward-backward passes before applying a single optimizer step. This effectively increases the batch size without requiring proportionally more GPU memory, which is essential when memory is limited.

question: What is the ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) strategy in DeepSpeed?
choices: A technique to train models with zero data preprocessing
An optimizer that sets all gradients to zero after each step
A memory optimization that partitions optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs to eliminate redundancy
A pruning technique that zeros out unimportant weights
explanation: ZeRO partitions optimizer states (Stage 1), gradients (Stage 2), and model parameters (Stage 3) across data-parallel GPUs rather than replicating them. This eliminates memory redundancy and enables training models that would not fit on a single GPU.

question: What is gradient clipping used for during LLM training?
choices: To increase the learning rate when gradients are small
To add noise to gradients for better generalization
To cap the gradient norm at a maximum value, preventing exploding gradients from destabilizing training
To remove gradients for frozen parameters
explanation: Gradient clipping limits the magnitude of gradients to a specified maximum value. This prevents sudden large gradient updates that can cause training instability or divergence, which is especially important in deep transformer models.

question: What is the 'rank' parameter in LoRA and how does it affect fine-tuning?
choices: It determines the number of GPUs used for training
It sets the priority of the training job in a cluster
It ranks the importance of different training examples
It defines the dimensionality of the low-rank decomposition; higher rank increases capacity but also parameters
explanation: The rank in LoRA determines the inner dimension of the decomposed matrices A and B. A higher rank captures more complex adaptations but increases trainable parameters. Common values range from 4 to 64, with diminishing returns beyond certain thresholds.

question: What is 'instruction tuning' in the context of LLM development?
choices: Optimizing the hardware instruction set for faster training
Compiling the model into machine instructions for deployment
Fine-tuning a pre-trained model on diverse tasks framed as instructions to improve zero-shot generalization
Teaching the model to output CPU instructions
explanation: Instruction tuning fine-tunes a pre-trained LLM on a collection of tasks described as natural language instructions. This improves the model's ability to generalize to unseen tasks by teaching it to follow the intent expressed in diverse instruction formats.

question: What is NVIDIA TensorRT-LLM designed to optimize?
choices: LLM inference performance on NVIDIA GPUs through kernel optimization, quantization, and batching strategies
Pre-training of LLMs from scratch on multi-node clusters
Image rendering for generative art applications
Network routing between data center nodes
explanation: TensorRT-LLM is NVIDIA's library for optimizing LLM inference. It provides custom CUDA kernels, in-flight batching, KV-cache management, quantization support, and tensor parallelism to maximize throughput and minimize latency on NVIDIA GPUs.

question: What is 'in-flight batching' (also called continuous batching) in LLM serving?
choices: Grouping all requests into a single batch before processing starts
Splitting a single request into multiple smaller batches
Batching requests by their source IP address
Dynamically inserting new requests into a running batch as existing requests complete, improving GPU utilization
explanation: In-flight batching allows new requests to be inserted into the processing batch as soon as existing sequences finish generating. This prevents GPU idle time that occurs with static batching, where the GPU waits for the longest sequence to complete.

question: What is model quantization in the context of LLM deployment?
choices: Increasing the number of parameters to improve accuracy
Splitting the model across multiple GPUs
Reducing the numerical precision of model weights (e.g., from FP16 to INT8 or INT4) to decrease memory and speed up inference
Converting the model from Python to C++ for faster execution
explanation: Quantization reduces the bit-width of model weights and optionally activations. Modern quantization methods like GPTQ and AWQ maintain most of the model quality while dramatically reducing resource requirements.

question: What does NVIDIA Triton Inference Server provide for LLM deployment?
choices: A model serving platform supporting multiple frameworks, dynamic batching, model ensemble, and concurrent model execution
A GPU hardware monitoring tool for temperature management
A training framework for pre-training LLMs from scratch
A web browser plugin for running models locally
explanation: Triton Inference Server is NVIDIA's production-grade serving platform. It supports multiple ML frameworks, provides dynamic batching, model ensembles, concurrent model execution, and metrics, enabling efficient and scalable LLM deployment.

question: What is speculative decoding in LLM inference?
choices: Using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens that the larger model then verifies in parallel, reducing latency
Randomly guessing tokens and discarding incorrect ones
Running multiple large models simultaneously and picking the best output
Precomputing all possible output sequences before the request arrives
explanation: Speculative decoding uses a small, fast draft model to generate several candidate tokens ahead. The large target model then verifies these tokens in a single forward pass. Accepted tokens skip individual generation steps, reducing overall latency.

question: Why is the 'time to first token' (TTFT) metric important in LLM serving?
choices: It measures the total cost of running the model
It measures how quickly the user receives the first token of the response, impacting perceived responsiveness
It measures the total number of tokens the model can generate
It measures the accuracy of the first generated token
explanation: TTFT measures the latency from when a request is received to when the first output token is produced. It includes prompt processing (prefill) time and directly affects user experience, especially in interactive applications.

question: What is PagedAttention, as introduced in vLLM?
choices: A technique that pages attention computations to CPU when GPU memory is full
A method for displaying attention heatmaps on web pages
A memory management technique that stores KV-cache in non-contiguous pages, reducing memory waste
A pagination system for splitting long documents into pages
explanation: PagedAttention manages KV-cache memory using a paging system similar to OS virtual memory. By storing KV-cache blocks in non-contiguous pages, it eliminates the memory waste caused by pre-allocating contiguous memory for variable-length sequences.

question: What is the difference between the 'prefill' and 'decode' phases in LLM inference?
choices: Prefill handles encoding and decode handles decoding in an encoder-decoder model
Prefill runs on CPU and decode runs on GPU
Prefill is compute-bound (processes all input tokens in parallel) while decode is memory-bandwidth-bound (generates one token at a time)
There is no difference; they are the same operation
explanation: The prefill phase processes all input prompt tokens in parallel, making it compute-bound. The decode phase generates tokens one at a time autoregressively, making it memory-bandwidth-bound. Optimizing each phase requires different strategies.

question: What is FP8 precision and why is it significant for LLM inference on H100 GPUs?
choices: An 8-bit floating-point format natively supported by the H100 Transformer Engine that doubles throughput vs FP16 with minimal accuracy loss
An integer format that replaces floating-point entirely
A format only usable during training, not inference
A proprietary format that only works with PyTorch
explanation: FP8 is an 8-bit floating-point format (E4M3 and E5M2 variants) that the H100's Transformer Engine supports natively. It doubles throughput compared to FP16 while maintaining sufficient dynamic range for accurate LLM inference.

question: What is model distillation in the context of LLM deployment?
choices: Removing unused layers from a trained model
Converting the model from one framework to another
Compressing the model weights using ZIP compression
Training a smaller student model to mimic the outputs of a larger teacher model
explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to reproduce the output probability distributions of a larger teacher model. This produces compact models suitable for deployment that retain much of the teacher's capability at a fraction of the computational cost.

question: What is GPTQ quantization?
choices: A training technique specific to GPT models
A GPU architecture for transformer operations
A one-shot weight quantization method using approximate second-order information to minimize quantization error layer by layer
A generative pre-training question-answering framework
explanation: GPTQ uses an approximate Hessian-based method to quantize model weights layer by layer in a single pass, minimizing the output error introduced by quantization. It enables efficient INT4 quantization of large models while maintaining quality.

question: What is 'prefix caching' in LLM inference?
choices: Caching the model weights on disk for faster loading
Storing and reusing the KV-cache for common prompt prefixes across requests to avoid redundant computation
Caching DNS prefixes for faster network routing
Pre-computing the full response for common queries
explanation: Prefix caching stores the KV-cache computed for shared prompt prefixes so they can be reused across requests. This eliminates redundant prefill computation when many requests share the same prefix, reducing TTFT and compute costs.

question: What is 'disaggregated serving' in LLM inference architecture?
choices: Serving the model from a single monolithic server
Separating the prefill and decode phases onto different GPU pools optimized for each phase
Breaking the API into microservices for each model layer
Distributing model weights across disk and memory
explanation: Disaggregated serving separates compute-bound prefill processing from memory-bandwidth-bound decode processing onto different GPU pools. Each pool can be optimized for its phase's characteristics, improving overall cluster utilization and efficiency.

question: What is structured output generation in LLM inference?
choices: Generating text in a fixed font and format
Organizing model weights into structured arrays
Creating structured training data from model outputs
Constraining the model's generation to follow a specific format (e.g., valid JSON) using guided decoding
explanation: Structured output generation constrains the LLM's token-by-token generation to conform to a specified grammar or schema. Techniques like constrained beam search or grammar-guided decoding mask out invalid tokens at each step, guaranteeing well-formed output.

question: What is AWQ (Activation-Aware Weight Quantization)?
choices: A training technique that adjusts activation functions
A way to quantize activations only, not weights
A quantization method that protects salient weights important for activations from aggressive quantization
An automatic workflow queue for model deployment
explanation: AWQ identifies the small fraction of weights that disproportionately affect activation magnitudes and protects them from aggressive quantization. By scaling these salient weight channels, AWQ achieves better quality than naive quantization at the same bit-width.

question: What is the role of a load balancer in LLM serving infrastructure?
choices: It balances the electrical load across GPU power supplies
It distributes incoming inference requests across multiple model replicas to optimize throughput and latency
It balances the training data across epochs
It equalizes the weights in the model layers
explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple model serving instances, preventing any single replica from being overwhelmed. It considers factors like current queue depth and GPU utilization to maintain latency targets.

question: In a RAG pipeline, what is the role of the embedding model?
choices: To generate the final text response to the user's query
To convert documents and queries into dense vector representations for semantic similarity search
To rank search results by keyword frequency
To translate documents between languages
explanation: The embedding model converts both documents (during indexing) and user queries (at query time) into dense vector representations in the same vector space. Semantic similarity between these vectors enables retrieval of relevant documents even without exact keyword matches.

question: What is 'chunking' in the context of RAG and why is it important?
choices: Splitting large documents into smaller segments before embedding and indexing to improve retrieval precision
Compressing documents to reduce storage costs
Encrypting documents for secure storage
Converting documents from PDF to text format
explanation: Chunking divides documents into smaller, coherent segments before embedding. Properly sized chunks improve retrieval precision by matching more specific passages and ensure retrieved content fits within the LLM's context window.

question: What is a vector database used for in RAG systems?
choices: Storing relational data in SQL tables
Running LLM inference on stored documents
Storing and efficiently searching high-dimensional embedding vectors using approximate nearest neighbor algorithms
Managing GPU memory allocation during inference
explanation: Vector databases store document embeddings and enable fast approximate nearest neighbor search. They use indexing algorithms like HNSW or IVF to quickly find the most semantically similar vectors to a query embedding at scale.

question: What is 'hybrid search' in the context of RAG retrieval?
choices: Searching across multiple cloud providers simultaneously
Combining dense vector similarity search with sparse keyword-based search (e.g., BM25) for better retrieval
Using both CPU and GPU for search operations
Searching both structured databases and unstructured text
explanation: Hybrid search combines dense retrieval (semantic similarity via embeddings) with sparse retrieval (keyword matching like BM25). This captures both semantic meaning and exact keyword matches, improving recall.

question: What is the purpose of a 're-ranker' in an advanced RAG pipeline?
choices: To re-sort the final generated text alphabetically
To rank GPUs by their performance
To re-order retrieved documents by relevance using a more accurate cross-encoder model before passing to the LLM
To randomly shuffle retrieved documents for diversity
explanation: A re-ranker (typically a cross-encoder) takes the query and each candidate document as a pair, producing a more accurate relevance score than the initial bi-encoder retrieval. This two-stage approach combines retrieval speed with cross-attention precision.

question: What is 'chain-of-thought' (CoT) prompting?
choices: Connecting multiple LLMs in a sequential pipeline
A hardware optimization for chaining GPU operations
A method for linking documents in a knowledge graph
A prompting technique that encourages the model to break down reasoning into intermediate steps
explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting adds phrases like 'think step by step' or provides examples with reasoning traces, encouraging the model to generate intermediate reasoning steps. This significantly improves performance on math, logic, and multi-step reasoning tasks.

question: What is an 'AI agent' in the context of LLM applications?
choices: A system where an LLM autonomously plans, uses tools, and takes actions to accomplish goals over multiple steps
A human employee who operates AI systems
A chatbot that only answers predefined FAQs
A GPU accelerator card
explanation: An AI agent uses an LLM as a reasoning engine that can plan multi-step actions, call external tools, observe results, and iterate until a goal is achieved. This extends LLMs beyond single-turn question answering to autonomous task completion.

question: What is NVIDIA NIM (NVIDIA Inference Microservices)?
choices: A GPU hardware monitoring tool
A training framework for small models
A pre-packaged, optimized microservice for deploying AI models with standard APIs, simplifying production deployment
A file format for storing embeddings
explanation: NVIDIA NIM provides pre-built, optimized container images for deploying AI models as microservices. NIM includes TensorRT-LLM optimization and standard API endpoints, allowing organizations to deploy LLMs to production with minimal infrastructure expertise.

question: What is 'grounding' in the context of LLM applications?
choices: Connecting the server to an electrical ground
Running the model on physical hardware instead of cloud
Training the model on ground-truth labels only
Anchoring the LLM's responses in verified external data sources to reduce hallucination
explanation: Grounding connects an LLM's generation process to external, authoritative data sources such as retrieved documents, databases, or APIs. This ensures responses are based on factual information rather than solely the model's parametric memory.

question: What is 'function calling' (or tool use) in LLM applications?
choices: The ability for an LLM to generate structured function call requests that an orchestrator executes against external APIs
Calling Python functions to load the model into memory
The internal function calls within the transformer architecture
A method for compiling LLM code into executable binaries
explanation: Function calling enables LLMs to output structured requests specifying a function name and arguments. The application then executes the function and returns results to the LLM, allowing it to interact with external systems and databases.

question: What is NVLink and why is it critical for multi-GPU LLM workloads?
choices: A high-bandwidth, low-latency interconnect between GPUs within a node, enabling fast tensor parallelism communication
A network protocol for connecting data centers across continents
A software library for compiling CUDA kernels
A display interface for connecting monitors to GPUs
explanation: NVLink provides direct GPU-to-GPU communication with much higher bandwidth than PCIe. This is critical for tensor parallelism in LLM training and inference, where GPUs must frequently exchange large activation tensors with minimal latency.

question: What is the NVIDIA DGX platform?
choices: A software-only solution for model training
An integrated AI supercomputing system combining multiple high-end GPUs, high-bandwidth interconnects, and optimized software
A consumer gaming GPU product line
A cloud-only service with no on-premises option
explanation: NVIDIA DGX systems are purpose-built AI servers that integrate 8 high-end GPUs connected via NVLink/NVSwitch, high-bandwidth networking, optimized storage, and pre-installed software designed to maximize performance for large-scale AI training and inference.

question: What is HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) and why is it important for LLM workloads?
choices: A type of CPU cache used for integer operations
A standard DDR5 memory module for servers
A 3D-stacked memory technology providing very high bandwidth, essential for feeding data to GPU compute units
A hard disk-based memory extension
explanation: HBM uses 3D-stacked DRAM dies connected via a silicon interposer to the GPU, delivering much higher bandwidth than traditional memory. The H100 uses HBM3 at 3.35 TB/s, which is critical since LLM inference is often memory-bandwidth-bound.

question: What is CUDA in the NVIDIA ecosystem?
choices: A parallel computing platform and programming model that allows developers to use NVIDIA GPUs for general-purpose computing
A type of GPU memory
A network protocol for GPU clusters
A file format for storing model weights
explanation: CUDA is NVIDIA's parallel computing platform and API. It allows developers to write programs that execute on NVIDIA GPUs, providing low-level control over GPU computation. Nearly all deep learning frameworks use CUDA for GPU acceleration.

question: What is the NVIDIA A100 GPU's key architectural feature for AI workloads?
choices: Dedicated ray-tracing cores for graphics rendering
Third-generation Tensor Cores supporting multiple precisions for accelerated matrix operations
Built-in CPU cores for hybrid computing
Integrated SSD storage for model weights
explanation: The A100's third-generation Tensor Cores provide massive throughput for matrix multiply-accumulate operations across multiple precision formats. This makes it well-suited for both training and inference of large AI models.

question: What is InfiniBand and what role does it play in LLM training clusters?
choices: A high-bandwidth, low-latency network fabric used for inter-node communication in GPU clusters
A type of GPU memory technology
A model compression algorithm
A Python library for distributed computing
explanation: InfiniBand provides the high-bandwidth, low-latency networking needed for gradient synchronization across nodes in large training clusters. RDMA support minimizes CPU overhead, making it the preferred interconnect for large-scale LLM training.

question: What is the purpose of NVSwitch in NVIDIA's GPU architecture?
choices: A software tool for switching between different CUDA versions
A power management system for GPUs
A high-bandwidth switch chip connecting all GPUs via NVLink, enabling any-to-any GPU communication at full bandwidth
A context-switching mechanism for GPU threads
explanation: NVSwitch is a dedicated switch chip that provides all-to-all NVLink connectivity between GPUs within a node. In a DGX H100 system, NVSwitch enables all 8 GPUs to communicate at full bandwidth, essential for efficient tensor and pipeline parallelism.

question: What is the significance of Tensor Cores in NVIDIA GPUs for LLM workloads?
choices: They are specialized for graphics texture mapping
They handle USB data transfer for the GPU
They provide mixed-precision matrix multiply-accumulate operations that dramatically accelerate transformer computations
They manage GPU fan speed and cooling
explanation: Tensor Cores perform fused matrix multiply-accumulate operations at very high throughput in lower precisions like FP16, BF16, and FP8. Since transformers are dominated by matrix multiplications, Tensor Cores provide massive speedups.

question: What is GPU memory bandwidth and why does it matter for LLM inference?
choices: The speed at which data is read from GPU memory, determining how fast model weights can be fed to compute units during the decode phase
The total amount of memory available on the GPU
The speed of PCIe connections between GPU and CPU
The bandwidth of the internet connection to the cloud
explanation: Memory bandwidth measures the rate of data transfer between GPU memory and compute units. During the decode phase, each token generation requires reading the full model weights, making memory bandwidth the primary bottleneck for throughput.

question: What is the NVIDIA Grace Hopper Superchip?
choices: A software tool for GPU monitoring
A GPU designed exclusively for gaming
A networking switch for data centers
A chip combining an NVIDIA Hopper GPU with a Grace ARM CPU via high-bandwidth NVLink-C2C
explanation: The Grace Hopper Superchip integrates an H100 GPU with a Grace ARM CPU connected by NVLink-C2C providing 900 GB/s coherent bandwidth. This eliminates the PCIe bottleneck, enabling efficient processing of models spanning CPU and GPU memory.

question: What is the role of NCCL in distributed LLM training?
choices: It provides optimized collective communication primitives (all-reduce, all-gather) for synchronizing data across GPUs
It manages the file system on GPU servers
It compiles CUDA code into machine instructions
It provides a graphical interface for monitoring GPU utilization
explanation: NCCL provides highly optimized implementations of collective communication operations essential for distributed training. It automatically selects the best communication topology using NVLink, PCIe, and InfiniBand.

question: What is the difference between A100 and H100 GPU architectures for LLM workloads?
choices: The A100 is faster than the H100 for all workloads
They have identical specifications and performance
The H100 only supports inference while the A100 only supports training
The H100 introduces the Transformer Engine with FP8 support and higher memory bandwidth, providing significant speedups
explanation: The H100 introduces the Transformer Engine that dynamically manages FP8/FP16 precision, fourth-generation Tensor Cores with higher throughput, HBM3 with 2x bandwidth over HBM2e, and advanced NVLink with 3x bandwidth versus the A100.

question: What is multi-instance GPU (MIG) on the A100 and H100?
choices: Running multiple games on a single GPU
Connecting multiple GPUs to appear as a single GPU
A feature that partitions a single GPU into up to seven isolated instances with dedicated memory and compute
A software emulator that simulates multiple GPUs
explanation: MIG partitions a single GPU into multiple isolated instances, each with guaranteed memory bandwidth and compute resources. This allows running multiple independent workloads on a single GPU with hardware-level isolation.

question: What is the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture's key advancement for LLM workloads?
choices: It removes GPU memory entirely and relies on CPU RAM
It is designed exclusively for computer graphics
It replaces CUDA with a new proprietary programming model
It introduces second-generation Transformer Engine with FP4 support and a dual-die GPU design
explanation: The Blackwell architecture features a dual-die GPU design, second-generation Transformer Engine supporting FP4 precision, and up to 192 GB HBM3e. These advances provide a significant leap in compute throughput and memory capacity for LLM workloads.

question: What is the purpose of NVIDIA cuDNN in the deep learning stack?
choices: A library of GPU-optimized primitives for deep neural networks including attention operations
A deep learning model architecture
A dataset management tool
A cloud deployment service
explanation: cuDNN provides highly optimized implementations of standard deep learning operations. Frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow call cuDNN for maximum performance on NVIDIA GPUs, particularly for convolution and attention operations.

question: Why is power consumption a critical consideration for large-scale LLM training clusters?
choices: GPUs consume negligible power compared to cooling systems
Power consumption is only relevant for consumer GPUs
Large GPU clusters consume megawatts, making energy cost and cooling major factors in total cost of ownership
Power consumption does not affect training performance
explanation: A single H100 can consume 700W, and a cluster of thousands of GPUs plus networking and cooling requires megawatts. Energy costs often rival hardware costs over time, making power efficiency a primary concern in data center planning.

question: Which TWO methods are commonly used to extend the effective context length of transformer models beyond their pre-training window? (Select TWO)
choices: Positional interpolation (scaling position encodings)
Reducing the model's hidden dimension
Sliding window attention or sparse attention patterns
Increasing the learning rate
Removing the embedding layer
explanation: Positional interpolation scales the position encodings to handle longer sequences. Sliding window or sparse attention patterns reduce the O(n^2) complexity by limiting each token's attention to a local window or sparse set of positions.

question: Which TWO properties of BF16 (bfloat16) make it preferred over FP16 for LLM training? (Select TWO)
choices: Same dynamic range as FP32 (8 exponent bits)
Higher precision (more mantissa bits) than FP16
Reduces risk of overflow/underflow during training compared to FP16
Uses 32 bits of storage per value
Requires special hardware not available on modern GPUs
explanation: BF16 maintains the same 8-bit exponent as FP32, giving it the same dynamic range. This larger range compared to FP16 reduces overflow and underflow issues, making BF16 more stable for mixed-precision training.

question: Which TWO benefits does subword tokenization (like BPE) provide over word-level tokenization? (Select TWO)
choices: It creates a fixed-size vocabulary that can represent any text including rare and unseen words
It always produces shorter token sequences than word-level tokenization
It handles morphological variations and misspellings more gracefully
It eliminates the need for embedding layers
It guarantees one-to-one mapping between tokens and characters
explanation: Subword tokenization maintains a fixed vocabulary while decomposing unknown words into known subword pieces. It also handles morphological variations by sharing common subwords, providing robustness to rare words and misspellings.

question: Which TWO factors primarily determine the memory required to store a pre-trained LLM's weights? (Select TWO)
choices: The total number of parameters in the model
The speed of the GPU's memory bus
The numerical precision used to store each parameter
The programming language of the implementation
The size of the training dataset
explanation: Weight memory equals the number of parameters times the bytes per parameter (determined by precision). A 7B parameter model in FP16 (2 bytes) requires approximately 14 GB, while the same model in INT4 (0.5 bytes) requires approximately 3.5 GB.

question: Which TWO are characteristic features of the transformer architecture that distinguish it from RNNs? (Select TWO)
choices: Parallel processing of all positions in the input sequence
Sequential processing one token at a time during training
Self-attention mechanism for capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence
Built-in memory cells like LSTM gates
Inability to handle variable-length sequences
explanation: Transformers process all input positions in parallel (unlike RNNs' sequential processing), enabling much faster training. The self-attention mechanism captures relationships between any two positions regardless of distance, avoiding the long-range dependency problems of RNNs.

question: Which TWO techniques are commonly used to improve the factual accuracy of LLM outputs? (Select TWO)
choices: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground responses in external knowledge
