Increasing the temperature parameter to maximum
Fine-tuning on high-quality, factually verified datasets
Removing all system prompts
Reducing the model size to minimum
explanation: RAG provides external, up-to-date knowledge at inference time to ground responses. Fine-tuning on high-quality factual data improves the model's internal knowledge and reduces tendency to hallucinate. Both approaches complement each other for improved accuracy.

question: Which TWO are key differences between encoder-only (BERT) and decoder-only (GPT) transformer models? (Select TWO)
choices: BERT uses bidirectional attention while GPT uses causal (left-to-right) attention
GPT has more parameters than BERT in all cases
BERT is pre-trained with masked language modeling while GPT uses next-token prediction
GPT cannot process any text input
BERT always uses more GPU memory than GPT
explanation: BERT uses bidirectional attention where each token attends to all others, suited for understanding tasks. GPT uses causal attention for autoregressive generation. Their pre-training objectives also differ: BERT predicts masked tokens while GPT predicts the next token.

question: Which TWO techniques help prevent overfitting during LLM fine-tuning? (Select TWO)
choices: Using dropout regularization
Increasing model size without limit
Early stopping based on validation loss
Training for as many epochs as possible
Disabling gradient computation
explanation: Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training, preventing co-adaptation. Early stopping monitors validation loss and halts training when it starts increasing, preventing the model from memorizing training data at the expense of generalization.

question: Which TWO are key components of the RLHF alignment pipeline? (Select TWO)
choices: A reward model trained on human preference comparisons
A convolutional neural network for image classification
A policy optimization algorithm (e.g., PPO) that updates the LLM using reward signals
A recurrent neural network for sequence processing
A decision tree for feature selection
explanation: RLHF requires a reward model trained on comparison data to predict human preferences, and a policy optimization algorithm like PPO that uses the reward scores to update the LLM toward generating outputs aligned with human preferences.

question: Which TWO are advantages of using NVIDIA NeMo for LLM training? (Select TWO)
choices: Built-in support for multi-GPU and multi-node distributed training with 3D parallelism
It only supports models with fewer than 1 billion parameters
Integration with NVIDIA's hardware ecosystem for optimized performance
It replaces the need for any GPUs
It only works with the English language
explanation: NeMo provides native support for distributed training with tensor, pipeline, and data parallelism. Its tight integration with NVIDIA hardware ensures optimized performance across the NVIDIA ecosystem.

question: Which TWO challenges are specific to training Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models? (Select TWO)
choices: Load balancing across experts to prevent some from being undertrained
MoE models require less total memory than dense models
Communication overhead from routing tokens to experts on different GPUs
MoE models cannot use the transformer architecture
MoE models do not benefit from pre-training
explanation: MoE training requires careful load balancing to ensure tokens are distributed evenly across experts. When experts reside on different GPUs, the token routing creates all-to-all communication overhead that must be managed.

question: Which TWO data quality practices are important for LLM pre-training? (Select TWO)
choices: Data deduplication to remove duplicate or near-duplicate documents
Using only data from a single source for consistency
Content filtering to remove low-quality, toxic, or harmful text
Keeping all duplicate content to increase dataset size
Avoiding any form of data preprocessing
explanation: Data deduplication removes redundant content that wastes compute and increases memorization risk. Content filtering removes low-quality, toxic, or harmful material that could degrade model quality and safety. Both are standard preprocessing steps.

question: Which TWO are features of the Megatron-LM framework for training large transformers? (Select TWO)
choices: Efficient tensor parallelism that splits weight matrices across GPUs within layers
It is designed exclusively for inference, not training
Pipeline parallelism that distributes model stages across GPUs with micro-batching
It only supports models up to 1 billion parameters
It cannot be combined with data parallelism
explanation: Megatron-LM implements efficient tensor parallelism for splitting layers across GPUs and pipeline parallelism for distributing model stages. These can be combined with data parallelism (3D parallelism) to train models with hundreds of billions of parameters.

question: Which TWO metrics are most important for evaluating LLM serving performance? (Select TWO)
choices: Tokens per second throughput across all concurrent requests
Number of parameters in the model
Per-request latency (including TTFT and total generation time)
Size of the training dataset
Number of layers in the model
explanation: Throughput measures overall serving efficiency and capacity. Per-request latency determines user experience. Both must be optimized together according to SLA requirements.

question: Which TWO are features of TensorRT-LLM for optimizing LLM inference? (Select TWO)
choices: In-flight batching for continuous request scheduling
Built-in model pre-training capabilities
Custom CUDA kernels optimized for transformer operations
Automatic training data generation
Integrated web browser for testing
explanation: TensorRT-LLM provides in-flight batching that dynamically adds new requests as existing ones complete. It also includes optimized CUDA kernels for transformer operations including fused attention and quantized matrix multiplication.

question: Which TWO strategies help reduce KV-cache memory during LLM inference? (Select TWO)
choices: Using GQA or MQA to reduce the number of KV heads
Increasing the model's hidden dimension
Quantizing KV-cache values to lower precision
Adding more transformer layers
Using a larger vocabulary size
explanation: GQA/MQA reduces KV-cache by sharing key-value heads across multiple query heads. KV-cache quantization reduces the memory per stored vector by using lower precision, achieving substantial savings with minimal quality impact.

question: Which TWO are common approaches to serving LLMs that exceed a single GPU's memory? (Select TWO)
choices: Tensor parallelism to split model layers across GPUs
Running the model entirely on CPU instead
Pipeline parallelism to assign layer groups to different GPUs
Reducing the context window to 1 token
Using only INT1 quantization for all weights
explanation: Tensor parallelism splits individual weight matrices across GPUs for intra-layer distribution. Pipeline parallelism assigns groups of consecutive layers to different GPUs. Both enable serving models that exceed single-GPU memory, with different latency and bandwidth tradeoffs.

question: Which TWO are benefits of using quantization for LLM inference deployment? (Select TWO)
choices: Reduced GPU memory usage allowing larger models to fit on fewer GPUs
Guaranteed improvement in model accuracy
Increased inference throughput due to smaller data transfers and faster computation
Elimination of the need for any GPU hardware
Automatic improvement in training convergence
explanation: Quantization reduces memory footprint by using fewer bits per weight, allowing larger models on less hardware. It also increases throughput since smaller data types require less memory bandwidth to transfer and can be computed faster on Tensor Cores.

question: Which TWO are advantages of using NVIDIA Triton Inference Server for production deployment? (Select TWO)
choices: Support for multiple ML frameworks (TensorRT, PyTorch, ONNX) in a single serving platform
It only works with NVIDIA consumer GPUs
Dynamic batching and model ensemble capabilities for optimized throughput
It requires rewriting all models in C++
It does not support any monitoring or metrics
explanation: Triton supports multiple frameworks, eliminating the need for separate serving infrastructure per framework. Its dynamic batching automatically groups requests for efficient GPU utilization, and model ensembles enable multi-stage inference pipelines.

question: Which TWO are common evaluation metrics for RAG answer quality? (Select TWO)
choices: Faithfulness (whether the answer is grounded in retrieved documents)
GPU thermal throttling frequency
Answer relevance (whether the answer addresses the user's question)
Model parameter count
Training data size
explanation: Faithfulness measures whether the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context. Answer relevance evaluates whether the response addresses the user's question. Together they form a comprehensive RAG evaluation framework.

question: Which TWO techniques improve retrieval quality in a RAG pipeline? (Select TWO)
choices: Query expansion to generate more effective search queries
Reducing the embedding dimension to 1
Metadata filtering to narrow search scope based on document attributes
Disabling the vector index entirely
Using a chunk size of 1 token
explanation: Query expansion reformulates or augments the query to improve recall. Metadata filtering uses structured attributes like date, source, or category to narrow the search space, improving precision.

question: Which TWO are benefits of NeMo Retriever in a RAG pipeline? (Select TWO)
choices: GPU-accelerated embedding generation and retrieval for high throughput
It eliminates the need for any vector database
Embedding models optimized for enterprise retrieval accuracy
It replaces the LLM generation component entirely
It only works with documents shorter than 100 words
explanation: NeMo Retriever provides GPU-accelerated embedding and retrieval for enterprise-scale RAG. It includes embedding models specifically optimized for retrieval tasks, improving semantic search accuracy versus general-purpose models.

question: Which TWO are common challenges when building production RAG systems? (Select TWO)
choices: Determining optimal chunk size and overlap for different document types
RAG systems always produce perfect answers without any tuning
Handling the lost-in-the-middle problem where LLMs underutilize context in the middle of the prompt
Vector databases never require any index management
Embedding models have unlimited context windows
explanation: Chunk size significantly affects retrieval precision and recall, requiring careful tuning per document type. The lost-in-the-middle problem shows LLMs focus on the start and end of context, requiring strategies for document ordering within the prompt.

question: Which TWO prompt engineering techniques can improve LLM output quality without changing model parameters? (Select TWO)
choices: Few-shot prompting with example input-output pairs
Reducing the model's hidden dimension size
System prompts that define the model's role and constraints
Quantizing the model to INT2
Removing all context from the prompt
explanation: Few-shot prompting demonstrates the desired behavior through examples, leveraging in-context learning. System prompts set role, tone, and constraints that guide the model's behavior. Both improve output quality without any parameter updates.

question: Which TWO are key benefits of the Transformer Engine for LLM training? (Select TWO)
choices: Automatic mixed-precision management that dynamically switches between FP8 and FP16
It eliminates the need for GPUs entirely
Significant speedup for attention and MLP operations in transformer layers
It only works with models smaller than 1 billion parameters
It replaces the need for distributed training
explanation: The Transformer Engine automatically manages per-tensor scaling between FP8 and FP16, maintaining accuracy while maximizing throughput. This provides up to 2x speedup for transformer operations by leveraging H100 FP8 Tensor Cores.

question: Which TWO factors determine how many GPUs are needed to serve a specific LLM? (Select TWO)
choices: The model's total memory footprint relative to per-GPU memory capacity
The color of the GPU server chassis
The required throughput and latency SLAs for the target concurrent users
The programming language of the API
The physical location of the data center
explanation: Minimum GPU count is determined by whether the model fits in available GPU memory. Beyond that, additional replicas may be needed to meet throughput and latency SLAs for the expected concurrent users.

question: Which TWO are challenges of scaling LLM training to thousands of GPUs? (Select TWO)
choices: Managing GPU failures and implementing fault tolerance with checkpointing
GPUs automatically becoming faster as more are added
Maintaining inter-node communication efficiency to minimize synchronization overhead
Training automatically becoming faster without optimization
Each GPU requiring its own dedicated internet connection
explanation: At scale, GPU failures become frequent, requiring robust checkpointing and recovery. Inter-node communication for gradient synchronization becomes a bottleneck requiring optimized collective operations and high-bandwidth interconnects.

question: Which TWO are key differences between PCIe and NVLink for GPU communication? (Select TWO)
choices: NVLink provides much higher bandwidth than PCIe for GPU-to-GPU transfers
PCIe is faster than NVLink in all scenarios
NVLink enables efficient tensor parallelism that would bottleneck on PCIe bandwidth
PCIe and NVLink have identical latency characteristics
NVLink replaces PCIe entirely in modern systems
explanation: NVLink provides up to 900 GB/s on H100 versus ~64 GB/s for PCIe Gen5, a 14x bandwidth advantage. This high bandwidth is essential for tensor parallelism where GPUs exchange large activation tensors every layer, which would bottleneck severely on PCIe.

question: Which TWO GPU specifications are most critical for LLM inference throughput? (Select TWO)
choices: Memory bandwidth (TB/s) for fast weight reading during the decode phase
Number of display outputs
Total GPU memory capacity (GB) for fitting model weights and KV-cache
GPU physical dimensions
Number of USB ports
explanation: Memory bandwidth determines how fast model weights can be read during the memory-bound decode phase, directly affecting per-token latency. Total memory capacity determines whether the model and KV-cache fit, and how large a batch can be served.

question: Which TWO are benefits of using DGX Cloud for LLM development? (Select TWO)
choices: Access to DGX-class performance without managing on-premises hardware
Free unlimited compute for any user
Pre-configured software stack optimized for AI workloads
Physical access to GPU hardware for custom modifications
No internet connection required
explanation: DGX Cloud provides on-demand access to DGX-class AI infrastructure without the capital expense and operational burden of on-premises deployment. The pre-configured software stack includes optimized frameworks and tools, reducing setup time for LLM development.

question: Arrange the following steps in the correct order of a single forward pass through a transformer decoder block:
items: Apply layer normalization to the input
Compute masked multi-head self-attention
Add residual connection from the input
Apply second layer normalization
Process through feed-forward network (FFN)
Add second residual connection
explanation: In a Pre-LN transformer decoder block, the input is first normalized, then processed through masked self-attention, followed by a residual addition. The result is normalized again, processed through the FFN, and a second residual connection is added.

question: Arrange the following stages of the typical LLM development pipeline in the correct order:
items: Data collection and preprocessing (including deduplication and filtering)
Tokenizer training on the target corpus
Pre-training on the large text corpus
Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on instruction-response data
Preference alignment (RLHF or DPO)
Evaluation and deployment
explanation: The LLM pipeline starts with data preparation, then trains a tokenizer. Pre-training learns general language patterns, SFT teaches instruction following, and preference alignment aligns outputs with human values. Finally the model is evaluated and deployed.

question: Arrange the following steps of a RAG pipeline in the correct order for answering a user query:
items: Receive and process the user's query
Generate a query embedding using the embedding model
Perform similarity search in the vector database
Construct a prompt combining the query and retrieved context
Send the augmented prompt to the LLM for generation
Return the generated response to the user
explanation: A RAG pipeline processes the query, converts it to an embedding, searches the vector database, constructs an augmented prompt with retrieved context, sends it to the LLM, and returns the response.

question: Arrange the steps for deploying an LLM using TensorRT-LLM and Triton in the correct order:
items: Export or obtain the pre-trained model weights
Optimize the model with TensorRT-LLM (quantization, kernel optimization)
Build the TensorRT-LLM engine for the target GPU
Configure the Triton model repository and settings
Launch Triton Inference Server with the optimized model
Validate inference correctness and benchmark performance
explanation: Deployment starts with obtaining weights, optimizing with TensorRT-LLM, building the engine, configuring Triton, launching the server, and finally validating performance.

question: Arrange the following GPU memory types from lowest to highest bandwidth:
items: System DDR5 RAM (accessed via PCIe)
GDDR6X (used in consumer GPUs)
HBM2e (used in A100 GPUs)
HBM3 (used in H100 GPUs)
GPU SRAM / L2 Cache
explanation: System DDR5 via PCIe has the lowest bandwidth (~64 GB/s). GDDR6X provides ~1 TB/s. HBM2e on A100 reaches ~2 TB/s, HBM3 on H100 reaches ~3.35 TB/s, and on-chip SRAM provides the highest at tens of TB/s.

question: Arrange the learning rate schedule phases in the correct temporal order during LLM pre-training:
items: Warmup phase (gradually increasing from near-zero to peak learning rate)
Stable phase at peak learning rate (optional)
Cosine decay phase (gradually decreasing from peak toward minimum)
Final minimum learning rate phase (constant near-zero rate)
explanation: LLM pre-training typically starts with warmup to stabilize early training, optionally holds at peak, then follows cosine decay to gradually reduce the learning rate, settling at a small minimum value.

question: Arrange the steps in building a RAG knowledge base (document ingestion) in the correct order:
items: Collect and preprocess source documents
Split documents into chunks with appropriate size and overlap
Generate embedding vectors for each chunk
Store embeddings and metadata in the vector database
Build or update the vector index for similarity search
explanation: Building a RAG knowledge base starts with collecting and cleaning documents, chunking them, embedding each chunk, storing in the vector database with metadata, and building the search index.

question: Arrange quantization precisions from highest to lowest model quality and memory usage:
items: FP32 (32-bit floating point)
FP16 / BF16 (16-bit floating point)
FP8 (8-bit floating point)
INT8 (8-bit integer)
INT4 (4-bit integer)
explanation: FP32 preserves the most information but uses the most memory. FP16/BF16 halves memory with minimal quality loss. FP8 and INT8 further reduce precision with increasing tradeoffs. INT4 provides the most aggressive compression.

question: Arrange NVIDIA GPU architectures in chronological release order from oldest to newest:
items: Volta (V100)
Ampere (A100)
Hopper (H100)
Blackwell (B100/B200)
explanation: NVIDIA data center GPU architectures progressed from Volta (2017) to Ampere (2020) to Hopper (2022) to Blackwell (2024). Each generation brought significant improvements in Tensor Core performance, memory bandwidth, and AI features.

question: Arrange the LLM inference steps in the correct order for generating a response:
items: Tokenize the input prompt into token IDs
Process all input tokens in the prefill phase
Generate the first output token from the output distribution
Cache key-value tensors in the KV-cache
Autoregressively decode subsequent tokens until a stop condition
explanation: Inference starts by tokenizing the input, processing all tokens in parallel during prefill, generating the first output token, caching key-value pairs, then decoding tokens one at a time using the KV-cache.

question: Match each transformer architecture type with its primary use case:
itemsRight: Text classification, NER, and sentence embedding using bidirectional context
Autoregressive text generation and conversational AI
Sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation and summarization
Scaling model capacity while keeping per-token compute cost manageable
explanation: Encoder-only models use bidirectional attention for understanding tasks. Decoder-only models use causal attention for generation. Encoder-decoder models map input to output sequences. MoE models route tokens to experts for efficient scaling.

question: Match each fine-tuning technique with its key characteristic:
itemsRight: Updates all model parameters, requiring memory for full optimizer states
Injects trainable low-rank matrices while freezing base model weights
Combines 4-bit base model quantization with low-rank adapter training
Prepends learnable soft tokens to the input, keeping all model weights frozen
explanation: Full fine-tuning modifies all parameters requiring maximum memory. LoRA adds small trainable matrices to frozen weights. QLoRA further reduces memory by quantizing the base model. Prompt tuning only trains continuous prompt embeddings.

question: Match each NVIDIA inference tool with its primary function:
itemsRight: LLM-specific optimization library with custom kernels, quantization, and in-flight batching
Production serving platform supporting multiple frameworks and dynamic batching
Pre-packaged inference microservice with optimized containers and standard APIs
Open-source engine using PagedAttention for efficient KV-cache memory management
explanation: TensorRT-LLM optimizes the computation. Triton provides serving infrastructure. NIM packages into easy-to-deploy microservices. vLLM innovates with PagedAttention for memory-efficient serving.

question: Match each NVIDIA GPU feature with its benefit for LLM workloads:
itemsRight: Accelerated mixed-precision matrix operations for attention and FFN layers
High-bandwidth memory for fast weight reading during the decode phase
High-speed GPU-to-GPU interconnect enabling efficient tensor parallelism
Hardware partitioning of a single GPU into isolated instances for multiple workloads
explanation: Tensor Cores accelerate the matrix multiplications central to transformers. HBM3 provides bandwidth for memory-bound inference. NVLink enables fast inter-GPU communication. MIG partitions a GPU for multi-tenant use.

question: Match each RAG component with its role in the pipeline:
itemsRight: Converts text into dense vector representations for semantic similarity
Stores and indexes embeddings for fast approximate nearest neighbor search
Rescores retrieved candidates for more precise relevance ranking
Synthesizes a final answer using retrieved context and the query
explanation: The embedding model creates vectors. The vector database stores and searches them. The re-ranker improves precision. The LLM generates the final response grounded in retrieved context.

question: Match each distributed training strategy with how it partitions work across GPUs:
itemsRight: Replicates the model on each GPU and splits the training batch
Splits individual weight matrices within layers across GPUs
Assigns consecutive groups of layers to different GPUs as pipeline stages
Places different expert FFN modules on different GPUs with token routing
explanation: Data parallelism replicates models and splits data. Tensor parallelism splits within layers. Pipeline parallelism splits by layer groups. Expert parallelism distributes MoE experts across GPUs.

question: Match each decoding strategy with its behavior during text generation:
itemsRight: Always selects the token with the highest probability at each step
Samples from the k tokens with the highest probabilities
Samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p
Maintains multiple candidate sequences and selects the highest-scoring one
explanation: Greedy is deterministic, always picking the top token. Top-k limits to a fixed number of candidates. Top-p dynamically adjusts based on cumulative probability. Beam search explores multiple paths simultaneously.

question: Match each quantization method with its approach:
itemsRight: One-shot weight quantization using approximate Hessian information to minimize layer-wise error
Protects activation-aware salient weights by scaling channels before quantization
Migrates quantization difficulty from activations to weights by smoothing outliers
Uses 8-bit floating-point with per-tensor scaling, natively supported by Hopper
explanation: GPTQ uses Hessian-based optimization. AWQ protects salient weights. SmoothQuant redistributes difficulty between weights and activations. FP8 leverages hardware-native support on H100.

question: Match each NVIDIA platform with its description:
itemsRight: An integrated AI server with 8 H100 GPUs connected via NVSwitch
Cluster management software for orchestrating multi-node AI training jobs
A superchip combining a Hopper GPU with a Grace ARM CPU via NVLink-C2C
A cloud-hosted AI supercomputing service providing DGX-class infrastructure on demand
explanation: DGX H100 is the physical AI server. Base Command manages training workflows. Grace Hopper is a CPU-GPU superchip. DGX Cloud provides DGX-class performance as a cloud service.

question: Match each LLM application pattern with its description:
itemsRight: Augments responses with information retrieved from external knowledge bases
Enables the LLM to invoke external APIs via structured output requests
Encourages the LLM to break down reasoning into explicit intermediate steps
An autonomous system that plans, acts, and iterates using an LLM as its core
explanation: RAG grounds responses in retrieved documents. Function calling connects LLMs to external systems. Chain-of-thought elicits step-by-step reasoning. AI agents combine planning, tool use, and iteration for autonomous task completion.
